meta.titleTemplate: UN 1361: Is Coconut Charcoal Dangerous Goods?
meta.descriptionTemplate: UN 1361 coconut charcoal is {{imdgLabel}}, Packing Group {{packingGroup}}. What the classification means, the self-heating risk, and why it is prevented — not insured.

h1: UN 1361: is coconut charcoal dangerous goods?
heroSubtitle: Yes. Coconut shell charcoal ships as UN 1361 — {{imdgLabel}}, Packing Group {{packingGroup}}. This page explains what that classification is and what it means for your shipment.

hero.ctaQuoteAria: Ask a compliance question on WhatsApp
hero.ctaContactAria: Send an inquiry about UN 1361 classification

intro.p1: This page sits under the {{brand}} Logistics pillar. UN 1361 is the dangerous-goods classification for coconut charcoal by sea — a timeless definition. For what changed under the 2026 rules, see the regulation page; here we define the cargo itself.

keyFacts.heading: Classification at a glance
keyFacts.definition: The dangerous-goods identity of coconut shell charcoal.
keyFacts.shtValue: On request — supporting evidence, not an exemption

whatIs.body: UN 1361 is the United Nations dangerous-goods identifier for "Carbon, animal or vegetable origin" — the entry that covers coconut shell charcoal, wood charcoal, and similar carbonized organic materials shipped by sea. Cargo declared as UN 1361 is {{imdgLabel}}, and the IMDG Code governs how it is packed, marked, documented, and stowed.

cargoTable.intro: The dangerous-goods data your carrier and broker need, in one place.

selfHeating.h2: Self-heating, fire risk — and how it is managed
selfHeating.body1: Class {{unClass}} means the material can self-heat: it may warm in contact with air and, in the wrong conditions, ignite without an external spark. Contained-cargo self-heating fires have occurred days after loading, which is why the classification exists and why carriers take it seriously.
selfHeating.body2: The answer is prevention. The Special Provision 978 controls — weathering, a packing-temperature limit, and container headspace — are designed to stop self-heating before it starts, and incidents are rare. Honesty matters here: self-heating fire is inherent vice and is not covered by marine cargo insurance. You cannot insure your way out of it — you prevent it, with the controls and a documented compliance record.
selfHeating.cargoLinkLabel: What insurance does and does not cover

sht.body: The Self-Heating Test (UN Test N.4) measures whether a substance self-heats. We provide the report on request as supporting hazard evidence. Since IMDG Amendment 42-24 it no longer exempts UN 1361 from dangerous-goods carriage — a point the regulation page explains in full.
sht.documentsLinkLabel: The SHT in the document pack

whyRefuse.body: Because it is Class {{unClass}} self-heating cargo, charcoal needs a carrier that accepts declared dangerous goods and the factory audits that go with it. Some major lines have stepped back from charcoal, which is why we book with carriers that do.

markings.body: Every export carton and container carries the {{unNumber}}, Class {{unClass}} dangerous-goods markings and placards.

countryEntry.body: UN 1361 sets how the cargo is classified and declared for the sea voyage; the customs duty, taxes and entry steps then depend on where it lands. These guides cover the import mechanics market by market.

media.alt: Sealed shipping container bearing a Class {{unClass}} dangerous-goods placard for UN 1361 coconut charcoal
media.caption: A sealed UN 1361 container with Class {{unClass}} placards.

items.a: Yes. Coconut shell charcoal is shipped as UN 1361, {{imdgLabel}}, Packing Group {{packingGroup}}. Since {{dgMandatoryDate}} it must be carried as declared dangerous goods — there is no compliant non-DG route by sea.

UN 1361 is the dangerous-goods entry for "Carbon, animal or vegetable origin". Class {{unClass}} is the hazard class for substances liable to spontaneous combustion — materials that can self-heat in air. Coconut charcoal is assigned Packing Group {{packingGroup}}, the lowest severity within the class.

items.q: What is the Self-Heating Test and what does it test?
items.a: The Self-Heating Test (UN Test N.4) holds a sample at an elevated temperature in a circulating-air oven and observes whether it self-heats. We provide the report on request as supporting hazard evidence. Since IMDG Amendment 42-24 a passing result no longer exempts the cargo from dangerous-goods rules.

items.q: Will my charcoal catch fire in the container?
items.a: It is very unlikely when the cargo is handled correctly. The risk is real — Class {{unClass}} cargo can self-heat — but the SP 978 controls (weathering, a packing-temperature limit, and container headspace) are designed to prevent exactly that, and incidents are rare. Self-heating fire is not insurable, so prevention and a documented compliance record are the real safeguard.

items.q: Why do some shipping lines refuse charcoal or charge more?
items.a: Because it is Class {{unClass}} self-heating dangerous goods. Some lines have stopped carrying it; those that do require declared DG booking and, often, a factory audit, and apply dangerous-goods surcharges. We book with carriers that accept it.
